verses.fhb_text: I have said these things to you to keep you from falling away.
verses.decision_notes: Jesus continues the farewell discourse with a stated purpose for what follows.

verses.fhb_text: They will put you out of the synagogues. Indeed, the hour is coming when whoever kills you will think he is offering service to God.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: "God" preserved (not Father-language) — persecutors' false belief, not Jesus's Father-identification.

Renderings:
• "service to God" — persecutors' theos is the generic deity of their misguided zeal; v.3 unpacks: they do not know the Father. Per FHB-1.2-1 dialogue preservation, rule governs who the text points to, not what a character believes about a deity they do not know.

verses.fhb_text: And they will do these things because they have not known the Father or me.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Jesus distinguishes the Father from himself — the explicit filial marker anchors the chapter.

Renderings:
• "the Father" — Jesus speaking about the Father; his Person-relation per FHB-1.2-1 v4.3 grammatical agreement.

Hebrew/Greek: ton patera (the Father) → "the Father"

verses.fhb_text: But I have told you these things so that when their hour comes, you will remember that I told you of them. I did not say these things to you from the beginning, because I was with you.
verses.decision_notes: Jesus explains why this teaching comes now, not earlier.

verses.fhb_text: But now I am going to him who sent me, and none of you asks me, 'Where are you going?'
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: "him who sent me" — the Father is implied antecedent (per vv.3, 10, 28), but Greek preserves the participial reference rather than naming.

Renderings:
• "him who sent me" — Greek ton pempsanta me preserved literally; Jesus's standard self-description of the Father as sender.

verses.fhb_text: But because I have said these things to you, sorrow has filled your heart.
verses.decision_notes: Jesus names what his disciples are feeling — sorrow at his departure.

verses.fhb_text: Nevertheless, I tell you the truth: it is to your advantage that I go away, for if I do not go away, the Helper, the Holy Spirit, will not come to you. But if I go, I will send him to you.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: First Paraclete reference in this chapter — establishing apposition per FHB-1.2-7(F).

Renderings:
• "the Helper, the Holy Spirit" — scripture's own apposition (John 14:26); first chapter occurrence carries the full naming, subsequent verses path-continue.

Hebrew/Greek: ho paraklētos (the Helper / Advocate / Comforter) → "the Helper"

verses.fhb_text: And when he comes, he will convict the world concerning sin and righteousness and judgment:
verses.decision_notes: Path continuity — "he" carries the Helper just established in v.7.

verses.fhb_text: concerning sin, because they do not believe in me;
verses.decision_notes: First item of the threefold conviction — sin's root is unbelief in Jesus.

verses.fhb_text: concerning righteousness, because I am going to the Father, and you will see me no longer;
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Jesus's righteousness is vindicated by his return to the Father.

Renderings:
• "to the Father" — Jesus speaking about the Father per FHB-1.2-1 v4.3 grammatical agreement.

verses.fhb_text: concerning judgment, because the ruler of this world has been judged.
verses.decision_notes: The Father has judged the prince of this world through the cross.

verses.fhb_text: I still have many things to say to you, but you cannot bear them now.
verses.decision_notes: Jesus restrains his teaching to what the disciples can presently carry.

verses.fhb_text: When the Spirit of truth comes, he will guide you into all the truth. For he will not speak on his own authority, but whatever he hears he will speak, and he will declare to you the things that are to come.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: "the Spirit of truth" — Jesus's own chosen title, preserved verbatim per FHB-1.2-7.

Renderings:
• "the Spirit of truth" — to pneuma tēs alētheias; not collapsed to "the Holy Spirit" because Jesus's relational naming is the point.

verses.fhb_text: He will glorify me, for he will take what is mine and declare it to you.
verses.decision_notes: The Spirit's ministry is to glorify Jesus — Trinity in relation, not Spirit-as-rival.

verses.fhb_text: All that the Father has is mine; therefore I said that he will take what is mine and declare it to you.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Father-Son shared possession — "all that the Father has is mine" is one of the strongest Father-Son unity statements in John.

Renderings:
• "the Father" — Jesus speaking about the Father, his Person-relation per FHB-1.2-1 v4.3.

verses.fhb_text: A little while, and you will see me no longer; and again a little while, and you will see me.
verses.decision_notes: Riddle-form anticipation of cross and resurrection.

verses.fhb_text: So some of his disciples said to one another, "What is this that he is telling us, 'A little while, and you will not see me, and again a little while, and you will see me,' and, 'because I am going to the Father'?"
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Disciples echo Jesus's "to the Father" phrasing as they puzzle the saying.

Renderings:
• "to the Father" — disciples quote Jesus; preserve his Father-language verbatim.

verses.fhb_text: So they kept asking, "What does he mean by 'a little while'? We do not know what he is talking about."
verses.decision_notes: Disciples' confusion preserved without smoothing.

verses.fhb_text: Jesus knew that they wanted to ask him, so he said to them, "Is this what you are asking yourselves, what I meant by saying, 'A little while, and you will not see me, and again a little while, and you will see me'?
verses.decision_notes: Headline: "said" not "answered" per FHB-9.14-1 — Jesus initiates this response without a preceding question to him.

verses.fhb_text: Truly, truly, I say to you, you will weep and lament, but the world will rejoice. You will be sorrowful, but your sorrow will turn into joy.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Sorrow-to-joy reversal — the heart-promise of resurrection.

Renderings:
• "Truly, truly" — amēn amēn preserved as Jesus's solemn doublet.

verses.fhb_text: When a woman is giving birth, she has sorrow because her hour has come. But when she has delivered the baby, she no longer remembers the anguish, for joy that a human being has been born into the world.
verses.decision_notes: Headline: Childbirth analogy carries the labor-to-joy promise of v.20.

verses.fhb_text: So also you have sorrow now, but I will see you again, and your hearts will rejoice, and no one will take your joy from you.
verses.decision_notes: Headline: The resurrection promise — joy no one can take.

verses.fhb_text: In that day you will ask nothing of me. Truly, truly, I say to you, whatever you ask the Father in my name, he will give it to you.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The new pattern of prayer — to the Father, in Jesus's name.

Renderings:
• "the Father" — Jesus naming his Father per FHB-1.2-1 v4.3.

verses.fhb_text: Until now you have asked nothing in my name. Ask, and you will receive, that your joy may be full.
verses.decision_notes: Asking-in-my-name introduced as the disciples' new prayer access — joy is the fruit.

verses.fhb_text: I have said these things to you in figures of speech. The hour is coming when I will no longer speak to you in figures of speech but will tell you plainly about the Father.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Plain speech about the Father is the goal — the veil of riddles is lifting.

Renderings:
• "about the Father" — peri tou patros; Jesus's stated mission to reveal the Father.

verses.fhb_text: In that day you will ask in my name, and I am not telling you that I will ask the Father on your behalf,
verses.decision_notes: Jesus is not the reluctant intermediary — the Father himself receives them, v.27 will reveal why.

verses.fhb_text: for the Father himself loves you, because you have loved me and have believed that I came from the Father.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: "The Father himself loves you" — the climactic Father-heart line of the chapter.

Renderings:
• "the Father himself" — autos ho patēr; the Greek emphatic preserved.
• "from the Father" — para tou theou; NT "from God" construction distinguishing Theos from Christ resolves to Father-language per FHB-1.2-1 v4.2; Jesus speaker → "the Father."

Hebrew/Greek: autos ho patēr philei hymas (the Father himself loves you) → preserved
verses.footnote: Jesus tells the disciples the Father himself loves them — not at a distance, not through Jesus as reluctant middleman. Their love for Jesus and faith in his coming from the Father has placed them inside the Father's own affection.

verses.fhb_text: I came from the Father and have come into the world; now I am leaving the world and going to the Father.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The four-beat arc of Jesus's mission — from Father, into world, out of world, to Father.

Renderings:
• "from the Father" / "to the Father" — Jesus's eternal procession and return; both para tou patros and pros ton patera preserve the apposition.

verses.fhb_text: His disciples said, "See, now you are speaking plainly and not using any figure of speech.
verses.decision_notes: Disciples respond to v.25's promise — recognition that plain speech has begun.

verses.fhb_text: Now we know that you know all things and do not need anyone to question you. By this we believe that you came from the Father."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Disciples' confession echoes Jesus's own v.27-28 language.

Renderings:
• "from the Father" — apo theou; disciples speaking, distinguishing Jesus from Theos per FHB-1.2-1 v4.2 NT X-of-God; consistency with v.27's "from the Father" preserves the chapter's apposition.

verses.fhb_text: Jesus said to them, "Do you now believe?
verses.decision_notes: Headline: "said" not "answered" per FHB-9.14-1 — Jesus turns the disciples' confession back as a probing question.

verses.fhb_text: Behold, the hour is coming, indeed it has come, when you will be scattered, each to his own home, and you will leave me alone. Yet I am not alone, for the Father is with me.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Father is with Jesus even when the disciples flee — Father-Son unity through the passion.

Renderings:
• "the Father" — Jesus speaking about the Father per FHB-1.2-1 v4.3.

verses.fhb_text: I have said these things to you so that in me you may have peace. In the world you will have tribulation. But take heart; I have overcome the world."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Closing pastoral charge — peace in Jesus, tribulation in the world, victory already won.

Renderings:
• "take heart" — tharseite; modern English equivalent of "be of good cheer."

chapter_notes: John 16 is the third movement of Jesus's farewell discourse (chs. 14-17). The chapter is saturated with explicit filial markers (vv.3, 10, 15, 17, 23, 25, 26, 27, 28, 32) and extended Paraclete teaching (vv.7-15). Jesus is the speaker throughout; per FHB-1.2-1 v4.3 grammatical agreement, all Father-references render as "the Father" (Jesus's Person-relation) — not narrator's "our Father" or self-padded "my Father" except where the Greek emō patri demands it. Paraclete handling per FHB-1.2-7(F): first chapter appearance (v.7) establishes "the Helper, the Holy Spirit" apposition; v.8 carries path continuity with "he"; v.13 preserves Jesus's own title "the Spirit of truth." One new footnote category added — father_himself_loves_you at v.27 — naming what the FHB-distinctive emphasis on the Father's direct love accomplishes pastorally; the verse's autos ho patēr ("the Father himself") is one of the most tender Father-heart statements on Jesus's lips. "From God" constructions in v.27 (Jesus speaking) and v.30 (disciples confessing) both resolve to "from the Father" per FHB-1.2-1 v4.2 NT X-of-God clause, preserving the chapter's apposition consistency. "Service to God" in v.2 preserved as written — persecutors' false belief, not Jesus's Father-identification, and the v.3 contrast ("they have not known the Father") depends on the lexical preservation.
